data.title: Civeo Corporation: Building Up To A 300% Upside
data.description:
Editor's note: Seeking Alpha is proud to welcome The Buy Side as a new contributing analyst. You can become one too! Share your best investment idea by submitting your article for review to our editors. Get published, earn money, and unlock exclusive SA Premium access. Click here to find out more »

Introduction

As a full-time a long-term value investor searching for attractive companies for over a decade, it’s always nice to dive deep into predictable free cash-flow producing companies with a strong moat that are trading at a low price. With that in mind we look at Civeo Corporation (NYSE:CVEO) and walk through its market position, risks, current valuation based on their cash flows, shareholder structure and the expected shareholder value return. Given its leadership role in the modular structure industry, strong cash flow and tailwinds from stock buybacks planned, this value gem is a diamond in the rough.

Market Position

Civeo Corporation is an established leader in delivering temporary industry housing, and will undoubtedly be relevant in the long term considering its operations and market trends. Officially established in 2014 as a spin-off from Oil States International, the company began trading independently on the New York Stock Exchange under the ticker symbol CVEO on June 2, 2014, with its headquarters in Houston, Texas.

However, Civeo’s roots go back much further, as it brings together the experience of two predecessor companies:

  • In Canada, the story began in 1977 with Peace Trailer Industries (PTI), which initially rented mobile offices and accommodations for oil workers and later became one of the largest providers of workforce accommodations in North America.
  • In Australia, The MAC (ASX:MSL) was founded in 1996 and grew to become a leading provider of accommodations for workers in the resources sector.

Nowadays, Civeo Corporation specializes in providing workforce accommodations and hospitality services for the natural resource companies that are involved in oil, gas, coal, LNG, and iron ore production. Its core offerings include lodging, catering, housekeeping, facility management, and a range of support services. Civeo operates in key resource regions across Australia and Canada, providing long-term service agreements to deliver integrated solutions tailored to the needs of remote industrial operations.

Due to a recent acquisition of another four villages in the Australian Bowen basin, Civeo owns and operates a total of 28 lodges and villages in Australia and North America with an aggregate of approximately 27,500 rooms. In addition, Civeo operates and provides hospitality services at 22 customer-owned locations with more than 18,000 rooms.

The locations of their Australian villages are the following:

Meanwhile, their Canadian lodges are located here:

Interesting is their exposure to the full customer project life cycle. We can see here, that Civeo can cover the full project cycle of the customer and therefore generate high-quality recurring revenue.

It shows that revenues and predictable and stable. This is also reflected in their resilient revenue history over time:

Also, the well-capitalized customer base of Civeo is a positive thing to mention.

To get a quick picture of what one of these Civeo Villages looks like, here is one in Australia:

Outlook

The market outlook for hospitality service providers in the natural resources sector is cautiously optimistic for 2025. While commodity-driven demand is expected to remain steady, growth will be moderate, and operators must navigate cost pressures and heightened sustainability requirements.

For the Canadian market, Civeo is expecting headwinds due to macroeconomics and plans to reduce its workforce by around 25%. While this incurs a $1 million restructuring charge, the cost savings on labor are significant and would help Civeo weather the macroeconomic challenges its clients face.

On the other hand, is the Australian market further capitalizing on favorable market dynamics so that there will be strong occupancy levels and customer activity throughout their villages.

Therefore, management provides guidance for the full year 2025 to an expected revenue of $620-$650 million and $75-$85 million EBITDA.

Valuation

As value investors we want to buy great businesses for a lower price than what they’re worth. To calculate what a business is worth we have to take a look at its ability to generate free cash flow and put that into perspective with the price we must pay for these cash flows.

As Warren Buffett mentioned previously, “If you attempt to assess intrinsic value, it all relates to cash flows. The only reason for putting cash into any kind of investment now is because you expect to take cash out.”

Here he also refers to cash that is available for share repurchases, not only for dividends. Therefore, we can say that the most important thing to look at is free cash flow (FCF) available for repurchases or dividends. But there is one exception that should be adjusted for; normally FCF excludes the equity-based compensation since it's a non-cash expense. But for my valuation I treat equity-based compensation like the expense it really is for shareholders, due to its effect of share dilution.

Let’s take a closer look at Civeo's financials:

Here we see Civeo's strong ability to generate consistent free cash flow.

On average, Civeo was able to generate roughly $60 million annually over the last three years, which would be available for share buybacks, dividends, or repaying debt. This shows the company's resilience.

To put this into perspective with a share price, I calculate Civeo's Enterprise Value (EV) here.

I chose Enterprise Value because it is a particularly fitting way to determine this company's worth because it accounts for cash and debt, adding the net debt to the market cap. We obtain market cap by multiplying the total number of Civeo's outstanding shares by its current price per share.

Civeo's market cap: $20.92 x 13.453.260 shares outstanding = $281 million.

A market cap calculation completely excludes the debt the company still owes, which would reduce its ability to use free cash flow for shareholder return but rather have to devote more of it to service obligations.

Hence, we add the net debt position to the market cap to get a clearer picture of what the true price of the company is.

Net Debt = Total Debt − Cash and Cash Equivalents

Here we see the evolution of Civeo’s net debt over the last couple of years:

As we can see, because of its consistent ability to generate free cash flow, the net debt shrank from $351 million in 2016 to only $69 million as of the end of March 2025. That is quite impressive.

The market cap of $281 million and net debt of $69 million result in the current EV of $350 million.

But now let’s combine all that and calculate other metrics:

EV/FCF Is Key

Our most important metric here is EV/FCF, which stands at 5.9x currently. If inverted to 1/5.9, it implies a 17% total FCF return rate. As Charlie Munger said, "Invert, always invert."

Also let’s look at the net debt/FCF multiple of only 1.2x. This is important because we want to own companies that don’t owe a lot of debt. And particularly for Civeo that means they could pay off all their net debt with the FCF produced within around one year. That’s great. Following Warren Buffett's advice, I'm wary of companies with lots of long-term debt as it's sensitive to interest rate hikes and can significantly impact the predictability of cash flows and profits.

With the recent full year 2025 revenue and EBITDA guidance of $620 million to $650 million revenue and $75 million to $85 million EBITDA, these FCF levels are assumably sustainable for the foreseeable future.

Shareholder Structure

It's worth noting the shareholder structure here, as we get a sense of the ownership groups and interests that comes with it. First, we can say that institutional ownership interest is continuously growing in this company as we see in the following:

A more detailed look into the institutional ownership structure tells us that activist Investor Engine Capital took a large position in Civeo which accounts for about 9.8% of all outstanding shares.

Engine Capital sent a public letter to the board of directors, asking them to reevaluate the use of FCF due to its underperformance in share price and the opportunity to allocate this capital in a more shareholder-rewarding way via buybacks.

Additionally, management owns significant portions of the company, which is good and shows they are aligned with shareholder interests. Some of the largest shareholders are as follows:

Shareholder Return

There are two major ways of rewarding shareholders: Dividends and share buybacks. I'll examine them both.

Dividends

One way of returning capital to owners is through dividends. Civeo has not always distributed dividends, starting to do so just recently in 2023.

Personally, I think dividends can definitely be a way of returning capital to shareholders, but these must be cautiously evaluated, considering their sustainability and other uses of cash flow.

Share buybacks

Civeo also has a history of using FCF for buying back shares as we see:

I like seeing buybacks if the share price is low, because of the long-term value it creates.

We can't be certain about attributing the board's and management's recent decisions regarding dividends and buybacks to the letter from Engine Capital to the board of directors, or whether other reasons also had an impact. But we can say that Civeo announced in their Q1 earnings release that it will increase the repurchase authorization from 10% to 20% of outstanding shares, and after completion, to use 75% of FCF for buybacks, which is a great sign of commitment to shareholder return.

Fair value of Civeo

Given the recent Enterprise Value of $350 million and EV/FCF multiple of only 5.9x, Civeo definitely has room for price appreciation. So let's take a look at industry multiples:

We can see that the average EV/FCF multiple stands at 24.1x. If we compare that with Civeo's current multiple of 5.9x, we get a potential upside of 308%. That translates to a share price of about $65, give or take a few percent of that upside.

Risks and Uncertainties

To get a well-balanced overview of a company, we also must look at the risks and uncertainties that come with the business.

Commodity Price Exposure

Civeo’s top line performance is closely linked to the natural resources sector, particularly the oil and steel industry. This industry encompasses Civeo's main client concentration. Changes in commodity prices have a direct impact on client activity and capital investment, which influences the demand for Civeo’s workforce accommodations. This reliance on commodity prices creates a cyclical business model and therefore results in some revenue fluctuations. Civeo has no direct control over exposure to this risk.

Regulatory Framework

Civeo is operating in a complex regulatory framework itself stating that due to its operations in Canada, Australia, and the US, it has to comply with plenty of different regulators and environments. These regulations can change from time to time and are costly to comply with, which can therefore lead to unexpected expenses.

Competitive Pressure

Civeo operates in a competitive industry shaped by several critical factors. The competitive environment, like Fleetwood Corporation in Australia and can result in lower occupancy rates and pricing pressure, which may negatively impact Civeo's revenue and profitability. While Civeo is the dominant market player amongst its competitors in Australia, it faces competition mainly from Fleetwood Corporation, which offers temporary housing that overlaps with Civeo's product offering to mining industry and infrastructure clients. In Canada, competitors are mainly ATCO Structures and Logistics (ATC.X), Black Diamond Group Limited (BDI:CA), and Dexterra Group Inc. (DXT:CA).

Civeo maintains market dominance because its size and scale allow it to serve its target industry clients efficiently. It's able to lock in long-term revenue streams through the use of long-term contracts. Also, Civeo's value-add above its competition is in its comprehensive suite of services like facilities maintenance and even catering. Therefore, a risk to consider is if clients become more cost-sensitive and prefer a more à la carte approach, putting at risk both the long-duration nature of contracts and the cost of add-ons related to a full-service package.

Conclusion

Civeo Corporation is a solid player with a bright outlook, manageable risks, a management team that is aligned with shareholder interests, strong FCF, and a share repurchase plan in place, that will benefit shareholders long-term. I value this company right now as a long-term portfolio addition due to its unique position and economics. Given the difference between my valuation and its trading price now, I rate it as a Buy.

data.title: INmune Bio: XPro Trial Readout In Alzheimer's Disease Offers Big Opportunity
data.description:
Background

INmune Bio, Inc. (NASDAQ:INMB) announced Q1 2025 business and financial results on May 8th, 2025, beating the analyst’s estimates. More importantly, the company claims to be on track for sharing results of the MINDFul Phase II clinical trial in the second half of June.

The MINDFul trial is evaluating the safety and efficacy of XPro in patients with early Alzheimer’s disease (AD). XPro is a brain-penetrant, highly selective inhibitor of the soluble form of the Tumor Necrosis Factor ((sTNF)), a cytokine produced by activated microglial cells that is associated with the increased neuroinflammation and neurodegeneration observed in AD.

Unlike Leqembi (lecanemab) developed by Esai Co. (OTCPK:ESAIY) and Biogen Inc. (BIIB), and Kisunla (donanemab) from Eli Lilly and Co. (LLY), XPro’s mechanism of action has the potential to provide a safe therapeutic solution to patients with early AD, regardless of apolipoprotein E ε4 ((ApoE ε4) expression, and without increasing their risk of developing amyloid-related imaging abnormalities (ARIA). ARIA is a sign of swelling and/or bleeding of the brain, which is one of the major risk factors associated with the anti-amyloid treatments (e.g., Leqembi and Kisunla).

Interestingly, Tiziana Life Sciences Ltd. (TLSA), which is also targeting neuroinflammation, announced that foralumab induced a significant reduction in microglia activation in a patient with moderate AD (see image below). Suggesting its therapeutic potential in that patient group and supporting the scientific rationale of XPro’s mechanism of action.

Thesis

The Alzheimer’s disease global market is expected to grow at a 23% CAGR between 2023 and 2033, reaching up to $19.3 billion by 2033. Despite the large market size, and the highly unmet clinical need, early AD remains difficult to accurately diagnose and treat. Indeed, Leqembi and Kisunla have struggled to convince some regulatory agencies about their therapeutic advantage in light of the associated risk of developing ARIA. Resulting in labels limiting its use to patients with early AD who are non-carriers or carry only one copy (i.e., heterozygotes) of the ApoE ε4 gene. In this sense, INmune Bio estimates that XPro patient opportunity accounts for at least 70% of patients with early AD:

Based on the data INmune Bio and other companies presented at ADPD, we now believe more than two-thirds of early Alzheimer’s disease patients will be eligible for XPro based on ApoE4 status alone. I remind you that ApoE4 positivity was one of the four enrichment or enrollment criteria we used in the MINDFuL study.

The ApoE4 status of patients in the MINDFuL is almost identical to what is reported in recent major trials in Alzheimer’s. Patients with at least one ApoE4 allele make up more than two-thirds of the patients in these trials. This means the market opportunity for XPro in early AD has increased to nearly 70% of early AD patients, not the 40% we have previously been talking about.

Considering the highly unmet clinical need in early AD and the AD projected market size, I would expect that positive Phase II clinical trial results are likely to have a positive impact on INMB's share price, as well as raise interest from suitable acquirers/partners. Particularly if the MINDFul provides strong safety and therapeutic performance in early AD. Conversely, if XPro underperforms, INmune Bio might struggle to fund operations beyond its cash runway, Q3 2025.

INmune Bio is also developing CORDStrom, for the treatment of epidermolysis bullosa (EB). CORDStrom’s regulatory filings in the USA, Europe, and the U.K. are scheduled for 2026 (see image below). EB’s global market size is projected to reach $9.9 billion by 2032, with an 11.2% CAGR from 2025 to 2032. Nonetheless, given the short cash runway, I believe XPro’s clinical performance is going to be key to successfully funding operations beyond Q3 2025. Thus, this time around, I will not dive into CORDStrom or INKmune potential.

Based on XPro’s good scientific rationale, previous clinical performance, and Tiziana’s clinical results on microglia activation, XPro has the potential to deliver strong Phase II clinical results by mid-June, while CORDStrom may become commercially available by 2027, if successful at regulatory filings. Hence, I consider INMB a high-risk/high-reward speculative buy, supporting my “Strong Buy” rating for investors with high-risk tolerance.

Alzheimer’s Disease

Alzheimer’s disease is a degenerative neurological condition affecting the central nervous system, which impairs cognitive performance and ultimately can cause dementia in people with AD.

AD was first reported by Alois Alzheimer, a clinical psychiatrist and neuroanatomist in 1906. However, it was only by January 2023 when the first treatment aimed to slow the progression of AD, Leqembi, obtained its first regulatory approval in the U.S. Leqembi was later followed by Kisunla and obtained FDA approval in July 2024.

AD is usually divided by stages, including preclinical, mild cognitive impairment, mild dementia, moderate dementia, and severe dementia due to AD. The table below illustrates the different stages of AD, the symptoms, and associated biomarkers. Note the stages listed as mild cognitive impairment (MCI) and mild dementia due to AD are usually grouped as “early AD.”

To date, no treatment has been demonstrated to reverse the effects of AD, highlighting the importance of early diagnosis and supporting the need for novel treatments that target the root of the disorder and even restore the cognitive performance of people with AD.

In terms of causes, AD’s initiation and progression are usually associated with the formation and accumulation of amyloid-β ((Aβ)) plaques in the brain, which impairs neuronal communication and are the target of Leqembi and Kisunla. A second hallmark of AD is the accumulation of phosphorylated Tau, also known as pathological Tau ((P-Tau)).

Tau is an intracellular filament required for maintaining the structure and synaptic function of neurons. On the other hand, P-Tau is a damaged version of Tau, which forms intracellular bundles called “neurofibrillary tangles,” impeding the normal function and communication between neurons. P-Tau accumulation is highly correlated with the cognitive impairment observed in people with AD. P-Tau 217, in particular, can be identified and quantified from blood samples, and it is a very accurate marker for diagnosis and assessing the progression of AD. Note other companies, including Voyager Therapeutics (VYGR), are developing anti-AD therapies targeting P-Tau; check my latest coverage on VYGR and its competitive landscape here.

Neuroinflammation is the third hallmark of AD, which is present from the early stages of the disease, i.e., preclinical. Until a few years ago, researchers believed that neuroinflammation was only the consequence of Aβ plaques and P-Tau accumulation, but the most recent studies revealed the critical role of activated microglial cells and astrocytes in AD and neuroinflammation.

Although it remains unclear if neuroinflammation is one of the causes of AD, it is clear that neuroinflammation is associated with the spread of the P-Tau and Aβ plaques, becoming a significant driver of the disease and the link with the other two disease hallmarks.

Microglial cells, in particular, are considered a double-edged sword. They are the CNS immune cells, being in a resting state under standard physiological conditions while still monitoring all activity, supporting the healthy function of neurons and other CNS residents, and shifting into an activated state when stimulated.

The activated microglial cells and astrocytes are key initiators of neuroinflammation in AD and other neurodegenerative conditions. Activated microglial cells release inflammatory cytokines, which in turn attract and activate more astrocytes and microglial cells. This activity is not necessarily bad when temporarily activated microglial cells eliminate Aβ aggregates via phagocytosis. However, the continuous activation of microglial cells leads to sustained release of inflammatory cytokines, including sTNF, the reduction of the microglial cells capacity to eliminate Aβ and neurotoxins, the weakening of the brain-blood barrier that protects the CNS, the propagation of P-Tau and Aβ plaques, the disruption of the synaptic activity and death of neurons, and ultimately AD progression. Thus, highlighting the rationale behind tackling neuroinflammation and microglial activation as a therapeutic strategy against AD progression.

XPro

XPro 1595 is a brain-penetrant highly specific sTNF inhibitor, which is administered via subcutaneous injection. INmune acquired XPro’s global rights for development and commercialization from Xencor Inc. (XNCR) in 2017. XPro’s mechanism of action, see image below, enables it to neutralize sTNF by forming non-functional heterodimers while sparing transmembrane TNF ((tmTNF)).

This specificity is key to understanding the potential therapeutic advantage of XPro against AD. Soluble TNF, in the context of AD, can be described as the pro-inflammatory signal driving neuroinflammation and neurodegeneration, being responsible for shifting microglial activation from a regenerative to a pro-inflammatory state, inhibiting axon remyelination, inflammation and cell death. Meanwhile, tmTNF has the opposite effects, driving neuroprotection, healthy CNS immune activity, improves synaptic plasticity and remyelination of axons (see image below). In other words, XPro has the potential to restore the CNS immune response, blocking the exacerbated neuroinflammatory response commonly observed in patients with AD.

Building on this, in 2021, XPro’s Phase I clinical trial results demonstrated its capacity to reduce neurodegenerative and neuroinflammation markers in people with mild to moderate AD after 12 weeks of once-weekly subcutaneous injections. A posteriori analysis of samples obtained from the Phase I trial participants published last year demonstrated that XPro promotes the expression of proteins involved in axon regeneration, remyelination, and synapses.

Despite having the support of good scientific rationale and early clinical trial performance, the Phase II trial results are going to be key to understanding the therapeutic effect of XPro in patients with early AD. The MINDFul Phase II trial is expected to yield top-line results by mid-June. INMB’s management is very optimistic about XPro’s potential to demonstrate disease-modifying effects. Although so far the data is blinded, during the last earnings call the company reported on the safety profile in all cohorts:

On the call six weeks ago, I also mentioned the safety profile of XPro in the MINDFuL trial. Nothing has changed. There are no reports of ARIA. No patients have had unscheduled MRIs due to pNF symptoms or headache, etc., and there have been no deaths. So far, XPro is safe and well-tolerated in this patient population that has an average age of 73 years old, and many of them have a long list of comorbidities.

If confirmed, the safety profile will be advantageous in terms of XPro’s population opportunity, but without a doubt the most important outcome of this study will be related to XPro’s impact on cognition. In this sense, the company is putting significant effort into building and validating a novel scale system specifically designed for objectively quantifying the cognitive performance of people with early AD. This scale is EMACC; if you haven’t yet, I recommend this video, where they explained the EMACC in full. Briefly, the EMACC was specifically designed for assessing the disease progression or improvements in people with early AD, providing higher sensitivity and precision than the other scales that were designed for other stages of the disease (see image below).

Considering the objectivity and sensitivity of the EMACC (see image below), I believe that the scale will be able to discern any positive or negative changes in the cognitive performance of the study participants.

However, the FDA and other regulatory agencies are yet to accept or dismiss data based on the EMACC. In the case of being dismissed, the company would be forced to stick with CDR, which has a lower sensitivity to discern changes in early AD, as the primary endpoint in future studies.

Taking together XPro’s performance in preclinical studies and Phase I clinical trial, its mechanism of action reducing neuroinflammation and promoting neuroregeneration, I believe XPro is likely to meet the primary endpoint, showing significant therapeutic efficacy when measured with EMACC (see image below).

Building on the speculation of good Phase II performance, the company will be seeking to start Phase III trials as soon as the FDA clears the study design. Leading me to highlight the company’s short cash runway and financials in the next section.

Q1 2025 Financials

INmune reported Q1 2025 earnings last week. INMB is a clinical stage company; as such, it lacks significant product revenues, while the operating expenses are mostly associated with the cost of research & development. In this sense, the company reported a 257% total revenue increase in Q1 2025 when compared against the same period of 2024 (see image below), which was attributed to selling human mesenchymal stem cells ((hMSCs)) to third parties.

The operating expenses decreased 9.75% in Q1 2025, due to nearing the completion of the MINDFul Phase II clinical trial. Hence, contributing to an 11.6% net income increase and an EPS increase of 29% in Q1 2025 vs. Q1 2024. Finally, the company reported $19 million in cash and equivalents by the end of March 2025, which is 25.6% lower than the figures reported the year before.

I estimate INMB’s quarterly cash burn rate to be equivalent to the Q1 2025 operating expenses, $9.955 million. Then, considering the current cash and equivalent, the company’s cash runway is two quarters, which is in line with the management’s projections, i.e., having sufficient funds to cover operations until Q3 2025.

The short cash runway is unlikely to be a surprise for INMB’s investors. Nonetheless, it remarks on the importance of XPro’s clinical performance. Should XPro deliver strong clinical results, demonstrating safety and therapeutic efficacy outperforming Leqembi and Kisunla, INmune Bio is likely to have many suitable options to finance operations beyond Q3 2025. Among those, I would expect suitable partners and/or acquisition offers. On the other hand, should XPro deliver poor results, the company might struggle to find favorable funding opportunities, limiting their options to diluting shareholders and/or raising debt at unfavorable conditions.

Valuation

INMB shares are trading around $7.30, and have a $169 million market cap. The shares have observed significant volatility in the last week, amid the multiple headwinds and uncertainties negatively impacting the Healthcare sector, versus the positive impact of XPro’s Phase II results speculations. For instance, INMB observed a 15.6% surge between May 9th and May 13th, while the shares have fallen 9.6% in the last two days. However, the 47% share price surge year-to-date remarks INMB’s positive performance in the recent months.

The Wall Street analyst’s consensus is bullish on INMB, rating the shares with an overall Buy and a $23.4 average share price target in the next twelve months (i.e., 220% upside potential from the current price). Seeking Alpha’s Quant rating is in line with Wall Street, while the Seeking Alpha analyst’s consensus is rating the stock with a Strong Buy amid XPro and CORDStrom’s potential to succeed. Building on this, EPS projections are estimating an 18% EPS growth in 2025, a very slow growth in 2026 (1.46% 2026 EPS growth), while in 2027 and 2028 the EPS is expected to return to over 20% growth. Those projections are in line with the speculation of positive Phase II clinical results associated with XPro, as well as positive regulatory performance of CORDStrom, whose BLA submission is scheduled for H1 2026.

INmune’s pipeline performance, led by XPro, supports my speculative “Strong Buy” rating for high-risk tolerant investors. However, it is very important to highlight the high level of risk associated with such investment. Should XPro deliver unimpressive results, for instance, unclear clinical significance of XPro’s therapeutic capacity, the company is likely to struggle to fund operations beyond Q3 2025. Hence, interested investors should exercise caution and utilise risk management strategies.

Risks

Along this article, I have highlighted the short cash runway as one of the major risks to my bullish thesis. The Q3 2025 cash runway is the reason why I dismissed CORDStrom and INKmune’s potential in the current analysis. Should XPro deliver poor results, the other two product candidates will be almost irrelevant. Yet, the scientific rationale and XPro’s performance to date, support an optimistic outlook.

Should XPro beat expectations, not only INMB’s shares are likely to spike, but also INmune’s probabilities to receive very interesting offers from potential partners and/or acquirers that might result in high returns to investors.

Building on this speculation, once the company has secured funding, CORDStrom BLA submission, and INKmune’s performance in solid tumors will become very relevant for the company’s outlook. From the long-term outlook standpoint, INmune is likely to face strong pressures from other biopharmaceutical companies commercializing and/or developing products targeting AD. In this sense, should XPro yield the expected positive results, I would not be surprised if INmune gets acquired in the near future.

Beyond INmune’s specific risks, investors should be aware of the strong headwinds that the Pharma, Biotech and the overall Healthcare sector are facing in 2025. For instance, the numerous uncertainties associated with the changes in regulatory policies might negatively impact the development of INmune’s product candidates.

Conclusions

INmune Bio is expected to share the long-awaited results of the MINDFul by mid-June. This Phase II clinical trial is evaluating the safety, tolerance, and therapeutic efficacy of XPro in patients with early AD. CORDStrom and INKmune, are the other two product candidates in INmune’s pipeline. Both product candidates have shown promising results in clinical trials, with CORDStrom expected to file for regulatory approvals in H1 2026.

Despite CORDStrom’s and INKmune’s performance, my speculative “Strong Buy” rating is mainly supported on the expectation of XPro achieving the MINDFul primary and secondary endpoints, demonstrating strong therapeutic performance by significantly reducing neuroinflammation and improving cognition when compared against placebo.

Should XPro succeed in Phase II, demonstrating its therapeutic capacity and safety profile, then it would be plausible to expect the share price to surge, potentially reaching the Wall Street analyst average share price target, standing at $23.4. This speculation could support the speculation of attractive acquisition offers from larger biopharmaceutical companies and/or partnerships to co-develop XPro. Hence, securing funds that would extend the cash runway beyond Q3 2025, without diluting shareholders.

On the other hand, INmune is likely to struggle to fund operations beyond Q3 if XPro disappoints. Forcing the company to attempt to fund operations via shareholder dilutions and/or raising debt at unfavorable rates. In addition, the macroeconomic headwinds that the sector is facing this year might worsen the company’s chances of getting funding at favorable rates.

Overall, I believe XPro’s performance to date and scientific rationale support INmune Bio’s optimism about the upcoming Phase II clinical trial results and my “Strong Buy” rating for high-risk tolerant investors. Should investors consider increasing or starting positions in INMB, they should also consider the speculative nature of such investment based on potential clinical trial success.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: AAII Sentiment Survey: Pessimism Drops
data.description:
Pessimism among individual investors about the short-term outlook for stocks decreased in the latest AAII Sentiment Survey. Meanwhile, optimism and neutral sentiment increased.

Bullish sentiment, expectations that stock prices will rise over the next six months, increased 6.5 percentage points to 35.9%. Bullish sentiment is below its historical average of 37.5% for the 18th time in 20 weeks and is above 30% for only the sixth time this year.

Neutral sentiment, expectations that stock prices will stay essentially unchanged over the next six months, increased 0.6 percentage points to 19.7%. Neutral sentiment is unusually low and is below its historical average of 31.5% for the 43rd time in 45 weeks.

Bearish sentiment, expectations that stock prices will fall over the next six months, decreased 7.1 percentage points to 44.4%. Bearish sentiment is unusually high and is above its historical average of 31.0% for the 24th time in 26 weeks.

The bull-bear spread (bullish minus bearish sentiment) increased 13.6 percentage points to –8.5%. The bull-bear spread is below its historical average of 6.5% for the 19th time in 21 weeks.

This week’s special question asked AAII members what they think about the Federal Reserve’s decision to keep interest rates unchanged.

Here is how they responded:

  • It was the right move: 73.6%
  • They should have cut rates: 17.1%
  • They should have raised rates: 2.7%
  • Not sure/no opinion: 6.6%

This week’s Sentiment Survey results:

  • Bullish: 35.9%, up 6.5 percentage points
  • Neutral: 19.7%, up 0.6 percentage points
  • Bearish: 44.4%, down 7.1 percentage points

Historical averages:

  • Bullish: 37.5%
  • Neutral: 31.5%
  • Bearish: 31.0%

The AAII Sentiment Survey has been conducted weekly since July 1987.

data.title: Nvidia Can Win Big On Trade
data.description:
Nvidia Corporation (NASDAQ:NVDA) was one of the companies that benefited from the $600 billion in commitments secured during President Trump’s visit to Saudi Arabia. Despite significant turmoil on chip export and trade this year, I believe the deal secured in Saudi Arabia could be part of a broader rethink on U.S. export controls for chips, as I outline in this report.

Nvidia Announces Partnership With Saudi Arabian HUMAIN

Somewhat interestingly, the announcement of a strategic partnership between Saudi Arabia’s HUMAIN and Nvidia was not mentioned in the fact sheet released by the White House. As part of the partnership, Nvidia will provide 18,000 GB300 Blackwell chips to HUMAIN. The 18,000 chips are a first phase in a batch that should see 500MW being deployed. I estimate that the chips are worth $1 billion to $1.3 billion. 18,000 chips would be the equivalent to 500 server racks assuming that the rack consists of 36 Grace and 36 Blackwell chips with a total value for the racks of $1.5 billion. Fully scaled the partnership would be valued $11.5 billion.

Could There Be Positive Implications For China?

While the deals announced with Saudi Arabia are not necessarily a template for future trade deals, it does show two things:

  1. Aerospace and defense sales are a major opportunity to reduce trade deficit.
  2. Semiconductor sales provide a significant opportunity to reduce the trade deficit as well.

The U.S. has ramped up its chip export control in several phases. Saudi Arabia was one of the countries with updated license requirement as Nvidia shared in its 10K (page 37):

In October 2023, the USG announced new and updated licensing requirements that became effective in our fourth quarter of fiscal year 2024 for exports to China and Country Groups D1, D4, and D5 (including but not limited to Saudi Arabia, the United Arab Emirates, and Vietnam, but excluding Israel) of our products exceeding certain performance thresholds, including, but not limited to, the A100, A800, H100, H800, L4, L40, L40S and RTX 4090. The licensing requirements also apply to the export of products exceeding certain performance thresholds to a party headquartered in, or with an ultimate parent headquartered in, Country Group D5, including China. On October 23, 2023, the USG informed us that the licensing requirements were effective immediately for shipments of our A100, A800, H100, H800, and L40S products (removing the grace period granted by the official rule). Blackwell systems, such as GB200 NVL 72 and NVL 36 as well as B200 are also subject to these requirements and therefore require a license for any shipment to certain entities and to China and Country Groups D1, D4 and D5, excluding Israel. To date, we have not received licenses to ship these restricted products to China. Additionally, we understand that partners and customers have also not received a license to ship these restricted products.

We now see that Saudi Arabia and Nvidia have engaged in a partnership. Days prior to the announcement of the partnership, the U.S. walked away from the AI Diffusion Rule set to become effective on the 15th of May. The AI Diffusion Rule would essentially tier countries into three categories from most accessible to most restrictive countries for chip exports. Countries like Saudi Arabia, but also a bunch of other countries would fall in the second tier with tighter control while countries such as China, Russia, Libya and Venezuela would fall in Tier 3 which is the most restrictive tier. This would essentially mean there is no chance for export licenses to be obtained.

While the Department of Commerce announced that the AI Diffusion Rule would not go in effect, it did warn against the use of Huawei Ascend chips, and U.S. AI Chips still cannot be used for training and inference of Chinese AI models. So, the export controls are still in place, but not with the tiered system in mind. So, China is still targeted, and Nvidia faces a $5.5 billion charge in its results because of that.

Nvidia is modifying its H20 chip for the Chinese market. It is essentially a light version of the chip in the same way Nvidia rolled out LHR [Low Hash Rate] GPUs during the crypto mining hype. However, for the time being, China and the U.S. have frozen tariffs for a 90-day period. While I do not believe that export controls on chips will be fully eliminated, I do believe that that a positive trade discussion between China and the U.S. could be a net positive for chip sales.

Nvidia will provide its Q1 FY26 earnings towards the end of this month and it will be interesting to see any comments from Jensen Huang on the current trade environment and outlook including chip controls.

If the U.S. is truly interested in narrowing down the trade deficit with China, I do believe that semiconductor sales should be part of that story. The question remains whether the trade dollar is heavily weighing for President Trump or the undeniable AI threat that comes from China is.

Nvidia Valuation Hinges Tremendously On EV/EBITDA Multiple

To determine multi-year price targets, The Aerospace Forum has developed a stock screener which uses a combination of analyst consensus on EBITDA, cash flows and the most recent balance sheet data. Each quarter, we revisit those assumptions, and the stock price targets accordingly. In a separate blog, I have detailed our analysis methodology.

For Nvidia, there remains a significant degree of uncertainty, but applying the company median EV/EBITDA multiple provides some upside against FY25 earnings which I would consider to be relatively risk-free and 66% upside against FY26 earnings. Obviously, the estimates for FY26 and beyond are highly dependent on the trade and export control environment. However, if we let Nvidia trade in between the peer group implied target and the company median target we get a stock price target of around $156 providing 15.6% upside and if we only allow Nvidia to rise by half of the upside that is supported by the share price we get a $179 price target. So, for now I am applying a $156 price target while maintaining my Buy rating.

Conclusion: Nvidia Valuation Hinges On Trade Environment

Nvidia is trading above the peer group valuation and given the strength in the product line up this does make sense. Simultaneously, it also means that when there are more negative pressures investors will value Nvidia according to a lower multiple. I believe that even in such a scenario the stock has significant upside and I also believe that constructive trade talks with China should result in some benefits for Nvidia.

data.title: Allot's Rally May Not Be A Sustainable Turnaround Yet
data.description:
Allot Ltd. (NASDAQ:ALLT) has seen a 45% surge in share prices in the past month, a bulk of which came in the runup and follow through to the Q1 2025 results released recently. After months of underperformance on profitability concerns, revenue contractions and executional challenges, the Q1 2025 season has infused hope again. The market is currently viewing Allot positively because of revenue growth, stronger SECaaS performance and a strategic partnership with Verizon. Investing in ALLT now could benefit from a potential turnaround story in the making or could be a value trap. Let us dive in further to understand which way to go.

What is Changing

Below chart shows how the shares' fortunes dived along with revenue contraction from 2021. The chart is not updated for Q1 2025, but shows how Allot has been recovering and knocking on the doors of stagnant to minor positive revenue growth in the past year or so. Q1 2025 revenues show a ~6% YoY revenue growth which is progress but nothing to grab headlines with. Months of declining revenues will only be offset if Allot grows and at an accelerated pace for at least two more quarters. The strong continued growth in SECaaS is not a new theme. Q4 2024 reports a 49% increase in SECaaS revenues with an ARR increase of 43%. Q1 2025 reports these growth numbers at 49% and 53%, confirming the growth trend. The headlines look good, but I am looking for something bigger for a buy thesis, given the size of the rally.

Operating margins were positive for the second quarter in a row although there a sequential fall in Q1 2025. The continued positive margins look good otherwise. Gross margins have not changed notably in the past year and are now at 70%, Non-GAAP. R&D expenses have dropped by 17% and Sales and marketing costs have also dropped by 6% highlighting operational efficiencies.

A SaaS like pivot has been brewing at Allot for some time now. The pivots has shown sustained momentum this quarter. A move towards higher margin recurring revenue contracts has helped margins overall and started stabilizing cashflow. The change from legacy product sales has meant a better revenue mix infusing both topline and bottom sustained positivity.

In terms of operating cash flow, ALLT actually saw a decline QoQ to $1.7m in Q1 2025, while still in positive territory. QoQ declines due to seasonality and debt servicing render this decline not an immediate worry compared to the fact that it remains positive and shows a turnaround from the negative operating cash YoY.

Growth Levers

Allot provides network intelligence and cybersecurity solutions to telecom providers and enterprises. This helps telecom operators understand and manage data flow in their networks and protect users from cyber threats. Telephone companies gain by having the capability now to prioritize bandwidth and also offer SECaaS to its customers and enterprises.

There is competition to Allot's SECaaS offering Allot's Secure. A close competitor Sandvine focusses on traffic visibility and analytics. Allot differentiates itself with a SECaaS solution that is more scalable with no app installations (zero touch, seamless installation and mass market adaptability).

Competition from traditional antivirus solutions (vs the telco provided security), traction to more telecom companies beyond Vodafone and Telefónica , expansion beyond Europe and Latin America and regulatory adaptation in new geographies will remain key challenges to monitor.

Reading into Recent Price Action

Without much changes in margins and revenues QoQ, a one month return of 45% points to markets valuing a couple of factors. One, the signals on the topline growth and bottom line positivity are being now perceived as sustainable. Two, the pivot to ARR is getting a vote of confidence from the street. Investors have to keep in mind that along with lack of expansion across geographies, there is a key risk from concentration risks from a few key enterprise customers.

Risks to Buying

I am concerned about execution risks as the pivot to the recurring revenue model is relatively untested for growth and scale. Competition from larger players like Palo Alto Networks, Fortinet, and Check Point, could either put pressure on pricing and margins or increase executional risk. Being a small cap, exposure to rate sensitivities and macro headwinds/sentiments could also pressure ALLT (like it probably lifted prices as a part of a broad based rally post US China trade ceasefire). Lower R&D spend, though a boost to margins, don't augur well in a fast evolving network security landscape and exposes ALLT to tech obsolescence risks if it does not adapt and evolve.

How to Play ALLT

My advice is to not play at all. I feel ALLT is mispriced due to temporary market factors, a bounce back (with reasons, but not commensurate to gains seen) from the cyclical lows. Existing investors can consider offloading some of their holdings. Fresh investments should wait for several green shoots, notably further significant revenue expansion from large telco deals, preferably geographical. Currently the risk reward is asymmetric and corrections cannot be ruled out.

data.title: AMD's Profitability Trends: A Buy Signal For Savvy Investors
data.description:
I upgraded Advanced Micro Devices, Inc. (NASDAQ: AMD) from Hold to Buy on September 9, 2024, and I reiterated that Buy rating on November 4, 2024. Those recommendations have not worked out so well. The stock is down 26.05% from my first recommendation compared to the S&P 500's (SPX) 3.00% rise. The second recommendation is down around 27.00% compared to the S&P 500's 1.00% loss. My mistake was assuming that the market would award the stock a higher valuation based solely on its rapid data center revenue growth. However, the market likely wants higher profitability and free cash flow ("FCF") margins before awarding it a much higher valuation.

Fortunately, AMD is showing progress in improving its profitability. As the company shifts to higher-value products, it has expanded its gross margin year over year for the fifth consecutive quarter. The company has shown evidence of operating leverage, meaning AMD is leveraging its existing infrastructure and resources to generate more sales without a larger operating cost increase. As a result, its operating margin has also increased during the quarter. If the company can continue growing its profitability and FCF, while maintaining solid revenue growth, the stock should rise to much higher valuations over the next several years.

This article will discuss AMD's acquisition of ZT Systems, a provider of AI and general-purpose compute infrastructure for hyperscale providers, in March 2025. It will also review its first quarter fiscal year ("FY") 2025 results. The article will also discuss a few risks, valuation, and why AMD remains a buy.

The acquisition of ZT Systems for Rack-scale technology

Rack-scale computing is complex for people unfamiliar with computing and networking. It took me a long time to understand the concept. Rack-scale computing is a new approach to data center architecture that treats an entire rack (a physical enclosure housing multiple servers and other equipment) as a single, integrated computing unit, instead of treating one individual server as a computing unit. Managing and orchestrating computing, memory, and networking resources through Software-Defined Infrastructure allows for better resource sharing, scalability, and efficiency in a Rack-scale environment.

A significant advantage of Rack-scale architectures is that they are designed for scaling out a computing system, meaning that designers can increase computing capacity by adding more racks or components within a rack that work together to distribute the workload (horizontal scaling or scaling out). This way of scaling is more cost-effective, efficient, and often more resilient than adding more CPU, RAM, or storage to make a single server more and more powerful (vertical scaling or scaling up).

AMD's Chief Executive Officer ("CEO") Lisa Su said on the first quarter FY 2025 earnings call:

Earlier this quarter, we completed our acquisition of ZT Systems, adding world-class systems design expertise to complement our silicon and software leadership. With ZT, we can provide ready-to-deploy rack-level AI solutions based on industry standards built with AMD CPUs, GPUs, and networking, reducing deployment time for hyperscalers, and accelerating time-to-market for OEM and ODM partners. The team is fully engaged in already co-designing with key customers on Rack-level designs optimized for our upcoming MI400 series and working with customers and OEM partners to accelerate time-to-market for our MI350 series.

The trend among chip manufacturers like Nvidia (NVDA) and Broadcom (AVGO) is to sell more system-level solutions like Rack Scale, rather than just selling the "brains" (CPUs and GPUs). For AMD to compete more effectively, it also needed to sell more system-level solutions. AMD purchased ZT Systems because of its expertise in rack-scale. AMD can now work with ZT's knowledge to offer customers racks set up to run AMD products, which should help make AMD a more attractive option.

The press release announcing the acquisition stated:

AMD (NASDAQ: AMD) today announced the completion of its acquisition of ZT Systems, a leading provider of AI and general-purpose compute infrastructure for the world's largest hyperscale providers. The acquisition will enable a new class of end-to-end AI solutions based on the combination of AMD CPU, GPU and networking silicon, open-source AMD ROCm™ software and rack-scale systems capabilities. It will also accelerate the design and deployment of AMD-powered AI infrastructure at scale optimized for the cloud. AMD expects the transaction to be accretive on a non-GAAP basis by the end of 2025.

By combining the two companies' strengths, AMD aims to speed up the process of designing and deploying AMD-based AI infrastructure optimized for the massive scale required by cloud computing environments. Accretive by the end of 2025 means AMD believes that this acquisition will start adding to its non-GAAP profits by the end of 2025.

Company fundamentals

AMD's first quarter 2025 revenue increased 36% year-over-year to $7.4 billion.

Let's look at segment results.

The company's revenue in the Data Center segment increased 57% to $3.7 billion. AMD's Data Center segment consists of the EPYC line of CPUs (central processing units) designed for servers used in cloud computing and enterprise data centers, the Instinct series of GPUs designed for high-performance computing and artificial intelligence workloads in data centers, and supporting hardware and software such as networking components and software optimization tools. According to the first quarter FY 2025 earnings release, Data Center Revenue growth was "primarily driven by growth in AMD EPYC™ CPU and AMD Instinct™ GPU sales."

One thing that may confuse some people is that the company now reports Client and Gaming as one Segment. Chief Financial Officer ("CFO") Jean Hu gave the following explanation on the company's first quarter fiscal year ("FY") 2025 earnings call:

As a reminder, for comparative purposes, our first quarter fiscal year 2025 financial statement disclosures include the combination of our client and the gaming businesses into a single reportable segment to align with how we manage the business. We continue to provide distinct revenue disclosures for our data center, client, gaming, and embedded businesses.

The combined Client and Gaming segments produced 28% year-over-year growth. Separately, Client revenue increased 68% year-over-year to $2.3 billion. The Client segment consists of revenue generated from sales of Desktop CPUs, mobile CPUs, and Commercial PC Processors (Ryzen PRO). According to the first quarter FY 2025 earnings release, "strong demand for the latest 'Zen 5' AMD Ryzen™ processors and a richer mix" drove the Client segment's revenue growth during the first quarter. Ryzen processors are the primary CPU product line for AMD's Client Segment.

Gaming revenue decreased 30% year-over-year to $647 million. Gaming revenue comes from selling semi-custom SoCs (system on chips) to gaming console manufacturers like Sony's PlayStation and Microsoft's Xbox, as well as sales of Radeon Graphics Products for some desktop PC partners like ASUS, MSI, Gigabyte, and Sapphire. According to the first quarter earnings release, gaming revenue was down, "primarily due to a decrease in semi-custom revenue." CEO Lisa Su said about Gaming on the first quarter 2025 earnings call:

As higher Radeon graphics sales were more than offset by lower semi-custom sales. While our semi-custom SoC sales declined year-over-year, console channel inventories have normalized, and demand signals have strengthened for 2025. For PC gaming, we launched our Radeon 9070 series to strong demand, as our new RDNA 4 Architecture delivers leadership performance for mainstream gamers. First week sellout set a record, and was more than 10x higher than our previous best Radeon launch. Demand remains very strong, and we are working closely with our board partners to replenish inventory weekly and meet the sustained demand.

Embedded segment revenue decreased 3% year-over-year to $823 million. Embedded Segment sells the "brains" of products used in a vast array of industries and applications, including Industrial Control and Automation; Networking and Communications; Medical Imaging and Devices; Automotive infotainment systems and advanced driver-assistance systems; Aviation radar and avionics, and Edge Computing. The "brains" of embedded systems usually consist of CPUs, GPUs, Field-Programmable Gate Arrays (FPGAs), and Adaptive SoCs. CEO Lisa Su said on the first quarter 2025 earnings call:

Embedded demand continues to recover gradually. We expect improving demand in the test and measurement, communications, and aerospace markets will drive a return to growth in the second half of 2025.

A richer RYZEN processor mix means that AMD sold more of its newer Ryzen product line at higher prices, which helped the company to grow its gross margins.

AMD's non-GAAP gross margin expanded 140 basis points ("bps") to 54% in the previous year's first quarter. Although the company has made some gross margin improvements, its GAAP gross margin significantly lags that of its primary competitors, Nvidia and Broadcom, so the market may be reluctant to increase its valuation because of its lagging profitability.

The company's non-GAAP operating expenses increased 28% to $2.2 billion, and its GAAP operating expenses increased 16% to $2.9 billion. The company's revenue growth of 36% is growing substantially faster than its GAAP and non-GAAP operating expenses, a sign of positive operating leverage. Because of the operating leverage, operating income and margin should increase this quarter.

CFO Hu said on the earnings call about the operating expense, "We continue to invest aggressively in go-to-market activities and in R&D to address the significant growth opportunities ahead of us."

AMD's GAAP operating income increased $0.76 billion to $0.8 billion, and its non-GAAP operating income increased from $1.1 billion to $1.8 billion.

Although its GAAP operating margin expanded year over year from 1% to 11%, it still lags significantly behind its peers, Qualcomm (QCOM), Nvidia, and Broadcom. This gap in operating margin performance with its peers may be a significant reason the market is unwilling to value it at the same level as its peers.

AMD's non-GAAP operating margin expanded around three points year over year to 24%.

The company's GAAP diluted earnings-per-share ("EPS") increased 528% year-over-year to $0.44, beating analysts' estimates by $0.04. Its non-GAAP EPS increased 55% year-over-year to 0.96, beating analysts' estimates by $0.03. The following image shows the GAAP net income to non-GAAP net income reconciliation.

The largest difference between AMD's GAAP and non-GAAP numbers is the amortization of acquisition-related intangibles, which is $567 million. This non-cash expense accounts for the loss in value over their estimated useful lives of intangible assets such as patents, trademarks, and software acquired during a business acquisition. This amount makes sense since AMD has made five acquisitions over the last three years, the latest being ZT Systems on August 19, 2024.

The second most significant difference between GAAP and non-GAAP numbers is stock-based compensation ("SBC"), which is $364 million, a reasonable amount compared to its peers.

Company Name Quarterly SBC to revenue% % Annual SBC to revenue% %
Nvidia 3.36% 3.63%
AMD 4.89% 5.46%
Qualcomm 6.39% 6.80%
Broadcom 8.58% 11.13%

AMD ended the first quarter with $7.31 billion in cash and short-term investments and $4.164 billion in long-term debt. Its debt-to-equity ratio is 0.08, meaning the company conservatively finances its operations with more equity than debt. The company's debt-to-EBITDA ratio is 0.75, meaning its debt is low relative to its EBITDA, suggesting it can quickly pay its debt obligations. AMD's current ratio is 2.80, meaning it has enough liquid assets to pay off its short-term debt.

S&P Global stated the following on the company's bond ratings in January 2025:

We anticipate Advanced Micro Devices Inc.'s (AMD) continued share gains in its x86 product segments and robust growth from its AI-accelerated computing chip sales to support solid growth trajectory over the next two years, which should bode well for further margin improvements and free operating cash flow (FOCF) generation. Therefore, we raised all our ratings on AMD, including our issuer credit rating, to 'A' from 'A-.' We also raised our short-term and commercial paper ratings on the company to 'A-1' from 'A-2.'

S&P's raising the issuer credit rating from A- to A means it raised the long-term bond rating from a lower medium to an upper medium investment grade. The current rating means AMD has a strong ability to meet its financial commitments but may be more susceptible to the adverse effects of changes in circumstances and economic conditions than debtors in higher-rated bonds. A change in short-term debt ratings from A2 to A-1 has a similar meaning. It has raised its rating for short-term debt from a lower medium to an upper medium investment grade.

AMD's trailing 12-month ("TTM") cash flow from operations ("CFO") to sales is 12.46%, meaning the company generated $0.12 of CFO from every $1 of sales over the last year. This number lags its peers by a substantial margin.

AMD's first quarter TTM CFO is $3.495 billion, and TTM free cash flow ("FCF") is $2.753 billion.

AMD's MI308 AI accelerator chip became subject to export license requirements to China by the U.S. government on April 15, 2025, impacting revenue and gross margins in the second quarter and FY 2025. Chief Financial Officer Hu said about the company's financial outlook on the earnings call:

This [outlook] includes an estimated $700 million revenue reduction as a result of the new export license requirement. Despite this headwind, the middle point of our guidance represents 27% year-over-year revenue growth. For the full year 2025, we estimated the revenue impact due to the export license requirement to be approximately $1.5 billion.

So investors should expect a substantial revenue and gross margin headwind throughout the year. Management guided for second-quarter 2025 revenue of $7.4 billion +/- $300 million, beating analysts' estimates of $7.24 billion before the results were reported. After the company reported, analysts raised their estimates to $7.37 billion. If the company hits the midpoint of its guidance, it will produce 27% year-over-year revenue growth.

The export restrictions mean that AMD can no longer sell the MI308 GPUs intended for the Chinese market. This inventory becomes unsellable in its originally planned destination, requiring AMD to write down the value of its MI308 inventory. Chief Financial Officer Hu said on the earnings call:

We expect the second quarter non-GAAP gross margin to be 43%, inclusive of approximately $800 million in charges for inventory and related reserves. Excluding this charge, our non-GAAP gross margin would be approximately 54%. Non-GAAP operating expenses to be approximately $2.3 billion, which includes approximately $50 million in OpEx due to the addition of the ZT Systems design team. The financials for the ZT manufacturing business will be reported as discontinued operations starting in the second quarter. We expect net interest and other expenses to be $5 million due to the debt associated with the ZT Systems transaction. Non-GAAP effective tax rate to be 13%, and the diluted share count is expected to be approximately 1.64 billion shares, which includes 9 million shares related to the ZT transaction.

The market generally liked the earnings report. The stock rose 1.76% the day after the company released its first-quarter results. As of its closing stock price of $112.46 on May 13, 2025, the stock has risen 13.82% since then. Another reason for the stock to rise further is that management announced a new $6 billion buyback on May 14.

Valuation

AMD's price-to-sales (P/S) ratio is 6.053, well below its three-, five, and seven-year medians. If it sold at its three-year median, the stock price would be $131.40, a 27.77% rise above its May 9 closing price of $102.84.

The following table compares AMD's value to several chip designer peers based on a one-year Forward P/S ratio to estimated revenue growth. Generally, the market will award the highest valuation to the company with the highest FCF margin first, the highest operating margin second, and the fastest revenue growth third. The market sometimes values stocks based on intangibles like a moat, market share, financial stability, and other factors.

Company Name One-year forward P/S ratio One-year forward estimated revenue growth One-year Forward P/S ratio-to-estimated revenue growth TTM FCF margin TTM Operating Margin One-year forward EPS growth estimates
AMD 5.21 22.35% 0.23 9.90% 10.29% 21.16%
Nvidia 14.25 53.94% 0.26 46.62% 62.42% 47.57%
Broadcom 15.60 21.40% 0.73 38.01% 34.34% 36.19%
Qualcomm 3.66 11.74% 0.31 27.69% 27.21% 15.18%
Intel (INTC) 1.82 -4.96% -0.36 -24.18% -7.79 -------
NXP Semiconductor (NXPI) 4.01 -5.74% -0.69 13.88% 26.90% -11.95%

Intel and NXP have negative one-year forward estimated revenue growth because analysts expect both companies' revenue to decrease at the end of their fiscal years.

Nvidia deserves the highest valuation because it has the highest estimated revenue growth rate, FCF margin, and operating margin. However, the market values Broadcom higher than Nvidia. The market likely undervalues Nvidia's revenue growth, FCF generation, and profitability on a relative valuation basis. Since AMD is well below the Nvidia, Qualcomm, and Broadcom FCF margin and operating margin, the market may be unwilling to push AMD's valuation much higher until it vastly improves its profitability and FCF generation capabilities.

AMD's price-to-earnings ("P/E") ratio is 75.08, well above the Information Technology sector median of 26.34. Some may consider AMD overvalued based on where it trades above its sector median.

AMD's 2025 PEG ratio is 1.21 (forward P/E of 25.64 divided by analysts' estimated EPS growth of 21.16%). The market generally considers a stock in the range of 1.0 to 2.0 fairly valued, so some might consider AMD fairly valued in the price range of $84.85 to $169.70.

AMD's 2026 PEG ratio is 0.41, indicating that the market may undervalue AMD's estimated 2026 EPS growth rate. Suppose the stock's 2026 EPS was 1.0; it would be $248.98, a 142% rise above its May 9 closing price. However, a 2026 PEG ratio looks out around 18-19 months, and the market is unlikely to price that EPS growth until it gains more certainty about how AMD will perform in this era of tariffs and intense competition.

AMD's price-to-FCF is 61.01, well below its three- and five-year medians.

Suppose it sold at its three-year median, the stock price would be $142.56. Therefore, some might consider the stock undervalued.

Risks

For AMD to gain further market share in the data center, it will need to invest heavily in R&D for its Instinct chip to keep up with Nvidia GPUs' performance and feature advancements. The success of AMD's upcoming MI350 and MI400 series is critical for the market's perception of its chips being close enough to the performance of Nvidia's Blackwell chips to become a reasonable alternative AI chip to purchase.

Market.us forecasts the AI data center market to grow from $23 billion in 2023 at a compound annual growth rate (CAGR) of 31.2% to $341 billion in 2033. On the positive side, that's a solid tailwind for AMD's revenue growth. However, on the negative side, the market will have high expectations for AMD to grow revenue against stiff competition from Nvidia. Although AMD increased its data center revenue in the first quarter of 2025 by 57% year over year, the market may not award it a higher valuation for that revenue growth because it lags substantially behind its peers in converting that revenue growth into FCF and profitability.

AMD has several less profitable parts of its business that also act as deadweight to overall revenue growth, namely its Gaming revenue, down 28% year over year, and its Embedded Segment, down 3% year over year. If the company's forecasts for its lagging segments potentially rebounding fail to materialize, the market may be reluctant to push the valuation higher.

The company's recent success has also relied heavily on its solid performance in the Client (desktop and mobile CPUs) segment, which correlates to consumers' financial health and sentiment. Since the beginning of the year, the U.S. Consumer Sentiment number has dropped from over 70% to 52.20%. When this number goes below 50%, it signals very weak consumer confidence and a pessimistic economic outlook. The decline in this metric bodes poorly for future smartphone and computer sales, which could make AMD's total revenue and profitability numbers look worse in the short term, especially in comparison to its peers like Nvidia and Broadcom, which have a smaller focus on the consumer.

New export control limitations on MI308 shipments to China will negatively impact AMD's revenue, particularly in the second quarter of 2025. An analyst asked CEO Lisa Su about the impact of the loss of GPU shipments to China on the earnings call. She said:

The best way to answer that question is in Q2, [Data Center] is not going to grow year-over-year. Just given what we said about the $700 million [loss of GPU shipments] coming out of Q2 and how we had previously talked about the evolution. But we do believe that [Data Center] will grow year-over-year going forward in Q3 and Q4 certainly for us to do the full year with strong double-digit growth.

Some experts think that in the long term, it will be bad for U.S. companies like Nvidia and AMD to be unable to compete for AI chips in China. This inability will allow companies like Huawei to rise unopposed and eventually provide competition on a global stage.

Broadcom's increasing presence in custom AI chips (ASICs) for specific hyperscalers like Alphabet poses a competitive threat in the AI inference market. AI Inference AI refers to the process where an already trained AI model uses its learned knowledge to draw conclusions from new data. Nvidia's and AMD's general-purpose chips work well in training applications. However, for specific inference applications, some customers prefer ASICs because of their higher energy efficiency and reduced power consumption. Manufacturers like Broadcom can also manufacture ASICs to produce lower latency and faster inference speed than general-purpose chips. So, in addition to battling against Nvidia in general-purpose chips, it will need to invest and prove that its general-purpose GPUs can compete effectively against custom ASIC solutions from Broadcom and others in inference applications.

AMD must scale its business in the face of all the above risks and improve its profitability and FCF. If investors fail to see substantially better profitability and FCF generation, they may be unwilling to value the stock at a much higher price.

AMD remains a buy

The company showed progress in increasing its profitability in its latest earnings period. The purchase of a higher-value, higher-profitability ZT system should help the company continue increasing its profitability over the next several years. As the company continues to scale its data center business and several underperforming segments begin to rebound, investors should see a noticeable improvement in profitability and re-rate the company's valuation higher.

The company sells at an attractive valuation, and growth investors will likely begin showing interest in it as the demand for AI infrastructure helps boost its revenue growth and profitability over the next several years. I recommend this multi-year growth story as a buy for risk-tolerant growth investors.
