data.title: Embecta: Not Nearly As Cheap As The 4x Forward P/E Ratio Would Imply
data.description:
Diabetes management company Embecta (NASDAQ:EMBC) offers a fascinating case study for financial metric sleuths.

On the surface, Embecta seems incredibly cheap. If you bring up a financial website, for example, you're likely to see eye-popping valuation numbers. Here's Seeking Alpha's overview page for EMBC stock, for example:

Shares are trading at a reported 4.1x forward earnings, offer a 5.2% dividend yield, and the stock is down more than 40% YTD, thus representing a fat discount to recent prices. Seems like a bargain at first glance.

Moreover, diabetes management has been a fast-growing market for many years now. Embecta would appear to be in a promising industry while trading at a rock bottom valuation and it pays a large dividend as well. So, what's not to like? In this article, I'll show why the valuation picture is significantly more complex.

A Struggling Spin-Off

Embecta came into existence as a publicly traded company a few years ago via spin-off. Far larger corporate parent Becton, Dickinson & Company (NYSE:BDX) spun it off in early 2022, and EMBC stock has traded largely downward since then:

Becton, Dickinson, for its part, is a large diversified medical supplies and devices company. It was seeking to restructure its portfolio toward more growth-focused verticals, so spinning off a strongly cash flow positive but minimal growth business would help achieve that end.

As part of the spin-off transaction, Becton, Dickinson also left Embecta with $1.6 billion of long-term debt. Embecta has annual revenues of about $1.1 billion and a market capitalization of just $700 million today, so a $1.6 billion debtload is massive in proportion to the size and value of the underlying business.

This is a fairly common spin-off feature. The parent company gets to considerably improve its balance sheet in the transaction. Meanwhile, the spin-co was left with a debtload that was disproportionate to the size of its underlying operations.

Embecta Hasn't Found Any Operational Traction Since The Spin-Off

And this leads to the first sign of potential trouble. Embecta's revenues have trended lower since it began reporting results separately from its corporate parent. Oftentimes spin-offs are able to benefit from being a standalone company which can allocate resources in a nimbler fashion. Embecta hasn't yet delivered on that potential.

While Embecta's revenue decline has been moderate so far, this revenue chart represents a worrisome trend in light of its long-term debtload.

Embecta's core structural issue is that it offers a pen needle-based insulin delivery system.

In recent years, alternative delivery systems such as pumps have emerged which many patients prefer. Embecta had been working on its own insulin patch pump solution to keep up with competition, but management decided to shutter that program. Embecta's leadership ultimately concluded that this was a highly competitive field which was not going to be able to generate attractive returns on investment.

That's all fine and well. I respect a management team that acknowledges its lack of strength within a market and reduces investment there when appropriate. However, in this case specifically, that decision means that Embecta is now primarily tied to its legacy pen needle solution. By all accounts, this seems likely to be a declining category over time as more and more patients embrace other alternatives.

As the table above shows, pen needles make up more than two-thirds of total revenues at present. So, there's not a lot else to fall back on if pen revenues continue to decline.

Long story short, this could well be a classic melting ice cube scenario. Those can work out okay for value investors as long as the price is right. But come into the situation well aware that Embecta is likely to be a significantly smaller business in the future than it is today. The one exception there would be if significant M&A occurs. But barring that, without the insulin patch pump business, it's hard to see where organic growth would come from. Additionally, Embecta reduced its R&D spend over the past year, further decreasing the likelihood that it will innovate its way out of its current revenue decline.

If the company had a clean balance sheet, it'd be easy to return the profits from its business and make shareholders happy even as its long-term prospects were limited.

However, Embecta was spun off with $1.6 billion in long-term debt, and this has only modestly declined to $1.52 billion now:

As revenues drop, it seems likely that a larger percentage of the company's profits will end up going to pay interest rather than being available for dividends or share repurchases.

EMBC Stock Is Not At 4x Earnings On A GAAP Basis

But Ian, isn't Embecta trading at 4x earnings? If the stock is really that cheap, it should be able to earn back its market cap and plenty more before the pen needle business' decline becomes too pressing of a matter.

Unfortunately, there are major adjustments to the company's earnings. As the table below shows, Embecta is trading at 13x trailing earnings on a GAAP basis. The EV/EBITDA of 24x also suggests that this stock is more expensive than it might first appear:

It's common for high-growth companies to have much higher adjusted earnings than GAAP earnings due to costs such as stock-based compensation. It's a bit more unusual for firms with flat or declining top lines to have huge adjustments to their earnings.

What's going on with Embecta specifically? There are several adjustments to its earnings, some of which seem normal. However, I'd like to point out "one-time stand-up costs" which the company defines as follows:

"One-time stand-up costs incurred primarily include: (i) product registration, labeling, and brand transition costs; (ii) warehousing and distribution set-up costs; (iii) legal costs associated with patents and trademark work; (iv) temporary headcount resources within accounting, tax, finance, human resources, regulatory and IT; and (v) one-time business integration and IT related costs primarily associated with our global ERP implementation."

There's obviously room for interpretation here. But some of these expenses such as labeling products, paying lawyers for patent work, and hiring temporary employees seem like pretty normal costs of running a company. I am not particularly keen on backing these expenses out of Embecta's overall valuation picture.

Let's see how this applies in practice. In its most recent quarter, Embecta reported adjusted earnings of 70 cents per share, up three cents from the prior year period. On a GAAP basis, however, earnings were just 40 cents per share and fell meaningfully from the 50 cents reported in the prior year's period.

If you annualize out Q2's GAAP earnings of $0.40, that gets you full-year earnings of $1.60 per share. That is still cheap based on a $12 stock, but it's not the same sort of fire sale price that the adjusted P/E ratio would imply.

Another check for earnings quality is how well a company can convert its reported net income into cash flow. Embecta's cash flow from operations was a modest $51 million over the past 12 months:

At that rate, it would take Embecta an awfully long time to pay down its $1.5 billion in long-term debt, especially if it wishes to continue paying a dividend along the way as well.

How Much Of The Recent Struggles Are Transitory Rather Than Structural?

Management suggests that the recent double-digit decline in the pen market is not reflective of Embecta's longer-term prospects. The company sees specific factors such as a recent port strike and challenges associated with the implementation of an enterprise resource planning ("ERP") system as leading to the particularly weak recent results.

I accept that explanation to a degree. That said, it's hard to deduce just how much of last quarter's 9.8% revenue drop was due to these temporary factors as opposed to the company's total addressable market shrinking as pen needle users migrate to other products. I haven't seen many compelling arguments for why pen sales would meaningfully grow again in the future.

There's also the risk factor around GLP-1 drugs for obesity and diabetes management which could shrink the overall market regardless of delivery method.

Previously, a bullish argument for Embecta had been that while pen needle revenues would fall as a share of the market, the whole category was growing quickly enough that there was room for everyone to perform. The rapid adoption of GLP-1 drugs casts some doubt on that premise.

EMBC Stock Bottom Line

You can reasonably argue that EMBC stock is now at a significantly discounted price after its more than 40% year-to-date decline. Sentiment is poor. If the company could merely get back to reporting flat revenues for a year or two, that would probably be enough to cause a significant improvement in the company's perception, and it wouldn't be hard to imagine shares trading back up toward $20.

That said, I don't see this as nearly as obvious of a deep value play as the headline 4x forward adjusted P/E ratio would suggest. There's a massive debt load here at more than twice the company's market cap. A lot of future cash flows will have to go to servicing debt rather than being channeled to dividends or share buybacks. That's especially true given the weaker conversion of earnings into cash flow recently.

I've seen some investors suggesting the Embecta should raise its dividend while pen needle revenues remain quite strong. That said, when you really look at the cash flow picture, there aren't all that many extra funds to go around after paying interest and meeting the current dividend obligation. I'd really like to see the company pay down more of its debtload before committing to a higher dividend going forward.

I personally have a tiny EMBC stock position in my buy-and-hold portfolio that I received when it was split off from Becton, Dickinson. In retrospect, I probably should have sold at the time of the spin. Thankfully, at least, I have not added to my Embecta position since the spin off. And that's unlikely to change any time soon. I remain concerned about terminal value here. Until the company can stabilize revenues and show an improved cash flow generation picture, there is more risk here than value investors might appreciate.
